questions.question: Explain the zombie process and how it can be prevented.
keyPoints: A zombie is a terminated child process whose entry remains in process table until parent collects exit status
Parent must call wait()/waitpid() to collect exit status and remove entry
Using SA_NOCLDWAIT flag in sigaction() prevents zombies
Using double-fork technique or ignoring SIGCHLD also prevents zombies

questions.question: Differentiate between wait() and waitpid() system calls.
keyPoints: wait() blocks until any child terminates, waitpid() offers more options
waitpid() can wait for specific PID using first argument
waitpid() supports WNOHANG for non-blocking
waitpid() supports WUNTRACED for stopped processes
waitpid() can wait for process groups

questions.question: What is the purpose of SA_NOCLDSTOP and SA_NOCLDWAIT flags in sigaction()?
keyPoints: SA_NOCLDSTOP: prevents SIGCHLD delivery when child stops
SA_NOCLDWAIT: prevents child from becoming zombie on termination
SA_NOCLDSTOP is useful when only termination matters
SA_NOCLDWAIT automatically reaps children without parent involvement

questions.question: Explain the SIGCLD/SIGCHLD signal and its significance in Unix process management.
keyPoints: SIGCLD/SIGCHLD sent to parent when child terminates
Allows parent to collect child exit status
Prevents zombie processes when handled properly
Essential for proper resource cleanup and preventing process table exhaustion

questions.question: Describe the double-fork technique for preventing zombie processes.
keyPoints: Parent forks child, child forks grandchild, then child exits
Grandchild becomes orphan adopted by init
init automatically reaps grandchild when it terminates
Original parent doesn't need to wait for grandchild
Useful for creating daemon processes

questions.question: Write a SIGCHLD signal handler that properly handles multiple child terminations.
keyPoints: Use waitpid() with WNOHANG in a loop
Loop until waitpid() returns no more children
Use -1 as PID to wait for any child
Check return value > 0 for each child reaped
Handle ECHILD when no more children

questions.question: Explain the default behavior of SIGCHLD in Linux and its implications.
keyPoints: Default disposition is SIG_IGN (ignore)
Child exit status is discarded automatically
Zombies are automatically reaped by init
Parent need not call wait() to prevent zombies
Trade-off: exit status information is lost

questions.question: Write a C program that demonstrates proper handling of SIGCHLD signal using sigaction() and waitpid(). Include proper signal handler with WNOHANG to handle multiple child terminations.
keyPoints: Use sigaction() to set up signal handler with SA_RESTART
Write signal handler using waitpid(-1, NULL, WNOHANG) in loop
Fork multiple child processes
Child processes do work and exit with status
Parent handles multiple SIGCHLD deliveries properly
No zombie processes should remain
Clean exit handling and status collection

questions.question: Explain in detail the various states of a child process and the role of SIGCHLD in process termination. Include zombie, orphan, and adopted states.
keyPoints: Running state: child executing
Stopped state: child paused, SIGCHLD can be sent with SA_NOCLDSTOP disabled
Terminated state: child exited but not collected - becomes zombie
Zombie state: process table entry retained until wait() called
Orphan state: parent died before child
init adoption: orphaned processes adopted by init (PID 1)
init automatically calls wait() to reap orphans
SIGCHLD triggers collection of exit status

questions.question: Compare signal() and sigaction() for SIGCHLD handling. Explain why sigaction() is preferred in production code.
keyPoints: signal() behavior varies across Unix implementations
sigaction() provides portable, POSIX-standard behavior
sigaction() allows setting flags like SA_RESTART, SA_NOCLDSTOP, SA_NOCLDWAIT
sigaction() with SA_SIGINFO provides additional siginfo_t information
sigaction() handler remains installed after signal delivery
signal() may reset handler to SIG_DFL on some systems
sigaction() allows blocking other signals during handler execution
Better reliability and predictability for production systems

questions.question: Design a robust process management system that handles multiple child processes, prevents zombies, and properly collects exit status of each terminated child.
keyPoints: Use sigaction() with SA_RESTART for reliable signal handling
Implement signal handler with waitpid(-1, NULL, WNOHANG) loop
Maintain data structure (array/list) to track child PIDs
Collect exit status using waitpid(pid, &status, 0)
Handle different exit scenarios: normal, signal, crash
Implement proper error handling for fork() failures
Consider using poll/select for I/O from children
Clean shutdown handling for all children
